title: Bloc Architecture: Scalable Project Structure for Flutter Apps

meta.title: Bloc Architecture ‚Äì Scalable Flutter Project Structure (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to organize Flutter apps using Bloc architecture. Folder structure, clean architecture layers, repository pattern, dependency injection, and best practices for maintainable code.

content.value: If your Flutter app is growing and becoming hard to manage, a proper architecture is essential. Bloc architecture helps you organize your project into scalable layers, making your code clean, testable, and easy to maintain.
What is Bloc Architecture?

Bloc architecture refers to a structured way of organizing Flutter applications using the BLoC pattern (Business Logic Component). It goes beyond just using `flutter_bloc` ‚Äì it defines how to separate concerns into distinct layers: **presentation**, **business logic**, and **data**. This leads to code that is testable, maintainable, and scalable, especially for large apps with multiple features.
Data Flow in Bloc Architecture
UI ‚Üí Event ‚Üí Bloc ‚Üí Repository ‚Üí Data Source ‚Üí Response ‚Üí State ‚Üí UI
This unidirectional flow ensures that each component has a single responsibility and that data changes are predictable.
Why a Layered Architecture?

value: **Separation of concerns** ‚Äì UI is decoupled from business logic, which is decoupled from data sources.
**Testability** ‚Äì Each layer can be unit‚Äëtested independently.
**Maintainability** ‚Äì Changes in one layer (e.g., swapping an API client) don‚Äôt affect others.
**Scalability** ‚Äì New features follow the same pattern, reducing cognitive load.

content.value: Real-World Example

value: **Insurance form submission with validation** ‚Äì Handle complex multi‚Äëstep forms, validate fields, and submit to backend.
**Login & authentication flow** ‚Äì Manage loading, success, error states, and token storage.
**Fetching dropdown data from APIs** ‚Äì Load options from remote services with caching.
**Handling loading, success, and error UI states** ‚Äì Centralised state management for consistent UX.

content.value: The Three Layers

rows: Process events, transform data, emit states
Repositories, data sources (API, local DB), models

content.value: Dependencies flow inward: **Presentation ‚Üí Business Logic ‚Üí Data**. The UI knows about the bloc, the bloc knows about repositories, repositories know about data sources.
Recommended Folder Structure

lib/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ main.dart
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ app.dart                      # App entry point with MultiBlocProvider
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ core/                         # Shared code across features
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ constants/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ themes/
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ utils/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ widgets/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ features/                     # Feature-based modules
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ auth/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ presentation/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ pages/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ widgets/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ bloc/                 # Cubit/Bloc for this feature
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ auth_bloc.dart
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ auth_event.dart
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ auth_state.dart
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ domain/               # Optional: use cases, repository interfaces
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ entities/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ repositories/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ usecases/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ data/                 # Repository implementations, data sources
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ repositories/
‚îÇ   ‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ datasources/
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ home/
‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ presentation/
‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ bloc/
‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ ...
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ di/                           # Dependency injection setup (if using get_it)
This structure is inspired by **Clean Architecture** but simplified for Flutter. For smaller apps, you can omit the domain layer and put repository interfaces directly in the data layer.
Implementation Example: Authentication Feature
Let‚Äôs build an authentication feature following the layered architecture. We'll create a bloc, a repository, and a data source, and wire them together.
1. Data Layer
Start with an abstract repository interface (optional) and an implementation that uses a remote data source.

content.title: features/auth/data/datasources/auth_remote_datasource.dart
features/auth/data/repositories/auth_repository_impl.dart

content.value: 2. Domain Layer (Optional)
Define the repository interface and entities (plain Dart classes).

content.title: features/auth/domain/entities/user.dart
features/auth/domain/repositories/auth_repository.dart

content.value: 3. Business Logic Layer (Bloc)

content.title: features/auth/bloc/auth_event.dart
features/auth/bloc/auth_state.dart
features/auth/bloc/auth_bloc.dart

content.value: 4. Presentation Layer

content.title: features/auth/presentation/pages/login_page.dart

content.value: Dependency Injection
You can inject dependencies using `BlocProvider` and a service locator like `get_it`. Here‚Äôs how to set up the dependencies for the authentication feature.

content.title: di/injection.dart

content.value: Then, in your `main.dart`, call `setup()` before `runApp`, and use `BlocProvider` to provide the bloc to the widget tree.
Bloc Architecture vs Clean Architecture

Both share similar ideas: separation of concerns, dependency inversion, and testability. Bloc architecture is a **simplified adaptation** for Flutter, focusing on the UI‚ÄìBloc‚ÄìRepository flow. Clean Architecture adds an explicit domain layer with use cases and entities, and enforces strict dependency rules. For most Flutter apps, the structure presented here strikes the right balance between scalability and simplicity.
Performance Tips

value: **Use `Equatable`** ‚Äì Avoids unnecessary rebuilds by ensuring states are only considered different when values change.
**Split large blocs into smaller feature blocs** ‚Äì Keeps each bloc focused and reduces rebuild scope.
**Use `BlocSelector` for granular rebuilds** ‚Äì Rebuild only the part of the UI that depends on a specific piece of state.
**Avoid heavy logic inside UI layer** ‚Äì Keep business logic and data transformations in the bloc or use cases.

content.value: When Not to Use Bloc Architecture

value: **Small apps with simple state** ‚Äì Over‚Äëengineering can slow down development.
**Prototypes or quick demos** ‚Äì A simpler state management (like `setState` or `Provider`) is sufficient.
**When Cubit alone is sufficient** ‚Äì For many features, a plain Cubit without full layered architecture is enough.

content.value: Best Practices

value: **Use `BlocProvider` at the highest level needed** ‚Äì Provide blocs where they are required, not necessarily globally.
**Keep blocs focused** ‚Äì Each bloc should handle one specific feature (e.g., `LoginBloc`, not `UserBloc` that does everything).
**Use `Equatable` for states and events** ‚Äì Avoids unnecessary rebuilds and makes state comparison easy.
**Avoid business logic in UI** ‚Äì Let blocs handle it; UI only dispatches events.
**Use `BlocListener` for side effects** ‚Äì Navigation, snackbars, etc., not in `BlocBuilder`.
**Consider use cases for complex business logic** ‚Äì They keep blocs thin and testable.

content.value: Common Mistakes

value:
‚ùå **Putting API calls directly in bloc** ‚Äì Hard to test and swap data sources.
‚úÖ Use repositories.

‚ùå **Creating `BlocProvider` inside `build`** ‚Äì Recreates the bloc on every rebuild.
‚úÖ Use `BlocProvider.value` or define outside build.

‚ùå **Mixing presentation and business logic in one file** ‚Äì Violates separation.
‚úÖ Keep layers in separate folders.

‚ùå **Not handling loading/error states** ‚Äì Users see no feedback.
‚úÖ Always define and emit loading/error states.
‚ùå **Over‚Äëengineering with unnecessary layers** ‚Äì For tiny apps, a simpler structure is fine.

content.value: Next Steps
Before diving into architecture, make sure you understand [Bloc basics](/flutter/bloc-introduction). Then explore [testing blocs](/flutter/bloc-testing) and [advanced patterns](/flutter/bloc-events).
Conclusion

A well‚Äëstructured Bloc architecture makes your Flutter apps scalable, testable, and maintainable. By separating concerns into presentation, business logic, and data layers, you ensure that each part can evolve independently. Start with a simple folder structure and gradually adopt more advanced patterns like use cases as your app grows.

faq.question: Do I need to use the domain layer in Flutter Bloc architecture?
faq.answer: No. For small projects, you can skip the domain layer and have the bloc directly depend on repository implementations. For larger projects with complex business logic, the domain layer adds clarity and testability.

faq.question: How do I share a bloc between multiple screens in Flutter?
faq.answer: Provide the bloc at a higher level in the widget tree using `BlocProvider` (e.g., in the parent widget). Then all child widgets can access it via `context.read<MyBloc>()`.

faq.question: Should I use `get_it` or `BlocProvider` for dependency injection with Bloc?
faq.answer: Both can work together. Use `get_it` for non‚Äëbloc dependencies (repositories, services) and `BlocProvider` for blocs. This keeps the dependency graph clean and testable.

faq.question: How do I handle navigation based on state changes in Bloc?
faq.answer: Use `BlocListener` to listen for specific states (e.g., `AuthSuccess`) and perform navigation. Avoid using `Navigator` directly inside the bloc.

faq.question: What's the difference between Bloc architecture and Clean Architecture?
faq.answer: Bloc architecture is a simplified version of Clean Architecture for Flutter. Clean Architecture adds an explicit domain layer with use cases and entities, and enforces stricter dependency inversion. The structure in this guide is a practical adaptation.

quiz.question: What is the main purpose of the data layer in Bloc architecture?
options: Fetch and persist data from sources

Why would you use a repository pattern with Bloc?
options: To separate data fetching logic from business logic
